nodes.name: Art Movements
nodes.description: Major Western art movements from the Renaissance to the 20th century

items.prompt: Which art movement, originating in 14th-century Italy, marked a 'rebirth' of classical Greek and Roman ideals and emphasized humanism, perspective, and naturalism?
items.explanation:
Originated in Florence, Italy, 14th–16th century (building on proto-Renaissance roots from the 1340s)
Name means "rebirth" in French—revival of classical antiquity's art, philosophy, and science
Pioneered linear perspective (Brunelleschi), chiaroscuro, and anatomical accuracy
Key artists: Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, Raphael, Botticelli, Titian
Leonardo's Mona Lisa and Michelangelo's Sistine Chapel ceiling are defining works
Patronized heavily by the Medici family in Florence and the Catholic Church in Rome
High Renaissance (c. 1490–1527) is considered the peak; ended roughly with the Sack of Rome (1527)
items.cardFront: 14th–16th c. Italy, classical rebirth, perspective, humanism

items.prompt: Which art movement, flourishing in 17th-century Europe, featured dramatic lighting, rich color, intense emotion, and grandeur, often in service of the Counter-Reformation?
items.explanation:
Flourished c. 1600–1750, originating in Rome
Promoted by the Catholic Church as a response to the Protestant Reformation
Characterized by dramatic contrast of light and shadow (tenebrism), movement, and emotional intensity
Key artists: Caravaggio, Rembrandt, Bernini, Rubens, Velazquez, Vermeer
Caravaggio's radical use of chiaroscuro influenced virtually every Baroque painter
Bernini's Ecstasy of Saint Teresa exemplifies Baroque sculpture's theatrical emotion
Extended into music (Bach, Handel, Vivaldi) and architecture (Palace of Versailles)
items.cardFront: 17th c. Europe, dramatic lighting, Counter-Reformation grandeur

items.prompt: Which late 18th- to mid-19th-century art movement emphasized emotion, individualism, the sublime power of nature, and rebellion against Enlightenment rationalism?
items.explanation:
Flourished c. 1790–1850, originating in Germany and Britain
Reaction against the Enlightenment's emphasis on reason and the Industrial Revolution's mechanization
Prized emotion, imagination, the sublime, and untamed nature over classical order
Key artists: Delacroix, Turner, Friedrich, Goya, Constable
Delacroix's Liberty Leading the People (1830) is the movement's most iconic painting
Caspar David Friedrich's Wanderer Above the Sea of Fog epitomizes Romantic landscape painting
Spanned literature (Byron, Shelley, Keats), music (Beethoven, Chopin, Liszt), and philosophy

items.prompt: Which art movement, originating in 1860s France, emphasized light, color, and everyday scenes painted en plein air over precise academic detail?
items.explanation:
Originated in France, c. 1860s–1880s
Named (mockingly) after Monet's Impression, Sunrise (1872) by critic Louis Leroy
Painted outdoors (en plein air) to capture fleeting effects of light and atmosphere
Key artists: Monet, Renoir, Degas, Pissarro, Morisot, Cassatt
Rejected by the official Paris Salon; held independent exhibitions starting in 1874
Monet's series paintings (Haystacks, Water Lilies, Rouen Cathedral) explored light across time
Berthe Morisot and Mary Cassatt were leading female Impressionists in a male-dominated field
items.cardFront: 1860s France, light, color, en plein air painting

items.prompt: Which art movement (c. 1886–1905) extended Impressionism while rejecting its limitations, with artists like Cezanne, Van Gogh, Gauguin, and Seurat pursuing more structure, symbolism, or emotional expression?
items.explanation:
Active c. 1886–1905; term coined by British critic Roger Fry in 1910
Not a unified style—an umbrella for diverse reactions against Impressionism's focus on surface appearances
Key artists: Cezanne, Van Gogh, Gauguin, Seurat, Toulouse-Lautrec
Cezanne's geometric simplification of form directly influenced Cubism
Van Gogh's expressive brushwork and color anticipated Expressionism; he is widely said to have sold only one painting in his lifetime (The Red Vineyard), though records suggest a few additional sales
Seurat invented Pointillism—tiny dots of color that blend optically (A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte)
Gauguin left France for Tahiti seeking "primitive" subject matter, influencing Primitivism and Symbolism
items.cardFront: c. 1886–1905, beyond Impressionism: Cezanne, Van Gogh, Gauguin, Seurat

links.text: c. 1886–1905, beyond Impressionism: Cezanne, Van Gogh, Gauguin, Seurat

items.prompt: Which early 20th-century art movement, originating in Germany, prioritized emotional experience and subjective feeling over realistic depiction, often using distorted forms and vivid colors?
items.explanation:
Originated in Germany c. 1905–1920s; term established by 1913
Two key groups: Die Brucke (The Bridge, Dresden, 1905) and Der Blaue Reiter (The Blue Rider, Munich, 1911)
Distorted forms, bold colors, and raw emotion to convey inner psychological states
Key artists: Kirchner, Kandinsky, Marc, Schiele, Nolde
Influenced by Edvard Munch (The Scream, 1893) and Van Gogh's emotional color
Extended into film (The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari, 1920; Nosferatu, 1922) and theater
Kandinsky, a Der Blaue Reiter founder, is traditionally credited with the first purely abstract painting (c. 1910–1913), though Hilma af Klint's abstract works may predate his by several years
items.cardFront: Early 20th c. Germany, distorted forms, subjective emotion

links.text: Early 20th c. Germany, distorted forms, subjective emotion

items.prompt: Which revolutionary art movement, pioneered by Picasso and Braque around 1907–1908, fragmented objects into geometric forms and showed multiple viewpoints simultaneously?
items.explanation:
Developed c. 1907–1914 in Paris by Pablo Picasso and Georges Braque
Picasso's Les Demoiselles d'Avignon (1907) is the proto-Cubist breakthrough
Abandoned single-point perspective—showed subjects from multiple angles at once
Two phases: Analytic Cubism (fragmented, monochrome) and Synthetic Cubism (collage, color)
Name coined by critic Louis Vauxcelles, who in 1908 described Braque as reducing everything to "cubes"
Influenced by Cezanne's geometric simplification and African/Iberian sculpture
One of the most influential art movements of the 20th century—paved the way for abstraction, Futurism, and Constructivism
items.cardFront: c. 1907–1914 Paris, Picasso and Braque, geometric fragmentation

links.text: c. 1907–1914 Paris, Picasso and Braque, geometric fragmentation

items.prompt: Which art movement, formally launched by Andre Breton's 1924 manifesto, sought to channel the unconscious mind through dreamlike imagery and irrational juxtapositions?
items.explanation:
Launched in 1924 with Andre Breton's Manifeste du surrealisme in Paris
Heavily influenced by Sigmund Freud's theories of the unconscious and dream interpretation
Key artists: Dali, Magritte, Ernst, Miro, Man Ray
Dali's The Persistence of Memory (1931)—melting clocks—is the movement's most recognizable image
Magritte's The Treachery of Images ("Ceci n'est pas une pipe") questioned perception and representation
Techniques included automatism (unplanned drawing), frottage, and exquisite corpse (collaborative writing/drawing game)
Breton was the self-appointed "Pope of Surrealism" who expelled members for ideological disagreements
items.cardFront: 1924 Paris, Breton manifesto, unconscious mind, dreamlike imagery

items.prompt: Which post-WWII art movement, centered in 1940s New York, was the first major American avant-garde movement and featured artists like Pollock and de Kooning?
items.explanation:
Emerged in New York in the 1940s; dominant through the 1950s
First major American art movement to achieve international influence—shifted the art world's center from Paris to New York
Two main strands: Action Painting (Pollock, de Kooning) and Color Field (Rothko, Newman)
Pollock's drip paintings (e.g., No. 5, 1948) made the physical act of painting the subject
Rothko's luminous color-field canvases aimed to evoke raw human emotion; he committed suicide in 1970
Also called the New York School
CIA secretly promoted AbEx abroad during the Cold War via the Congress for Cultural Freedom
items.cardFront: 1940s–1950s New York, Pollock, de Kooning, Rothko

items.prompt: Which 1950s–1960s art movement, led by Andy Warhol and Roy Lichtenstein, drew from mass culture, advertising, and consumer goods to blur the line between 'high' and 'low' art?
items.explanation:
Emerged in Britain mid-1950s, exploded in America early 1960s
Reaction against Abstract Expressionism's seriousness and elitism
Key artists: Warhol, Lichtenstein, Oldenburg, Rosenquist, Hamilton, Wesselmann
Warhol's Campbell's Soup Cans (1962) and Marilyn Monroe silkscreens are defining works
Lichtenstein enlarged comic-strip panels with Ben-Day dots, treating mass media as fine art
Richard Hamilton's 1956 collage Just What Is It That Makes Today's Homes So Different, So Appealing? is a key early work
Warhol's Factory studio became a celebrity gathering place; he survived a 1968 assassination attempt by Valerie Solanas
items.cardFront: 1950s–1960s, Warhol, mass culture, advertising as art

items.prompt: Which decorative art movement (c. 1890–1910) was characterized by flowing organic lines, natural forms, and the integration of art into architecture and everyday objects?
items.explanation:
Flourished c. 1890–1910 during the Belle Epoque
Characterized by sinuous, flowing lines inspired by natural forms—flowers, vines, insects
Integrated art across disciplines: architecture, furniture, jewelry, posters, glasswork
Key figures: Mucha (posters), Gaudi (architecture), Klimt (painting), Tiffany (glass), Horta (architecture)
Gaudi's Sagrada Familia in Barcelona is the movement's most famous architectural work
Klimt co-founded the Vienna Secession (1897), the Austrian branch of Art Nouveau
Known by different names across Europe: Jugendstil (Germany), Stile Liberty (Italy), Modernisme (Catalonia)
items.cardFront: c. 1890–1910, organic flowing lines, art in everyday objects

links.text: c. 1890–1910, organic flowing lines, art in everyday objects

items.prompt: Which decorative art movement (c. 1920s–1930s) featured bold geometric forms, rich materials, and sleek modernity, exemplified by the Chrysler Building in New York?
items.explanation:
Flourished c. 1920s–1930s; named after the 1925 Paris Exposition internationale des arts decoratifs
Characterized by geometric shapes, symmetry, bold colors, and luxurious materials (chrome, lacquer, ivory)
Applied to architecture, fashion, jewelry, furniture, film sets, and industrial design
Iconic buildings: Chrysler Building (1930) and Empire State Building (1931) in New York
The Chrysler Building, designed by William Van Alen, is often called the finest Art Deco skyscraper
Influenced by Cubism, Futurism, Egyptian art (after Tutankhamun's tomb discovery, 1922), and machine-age aesthetics
Experienced a major revival in the 1980s in Miami's South Beach district
items.cardFront: 1920s–1930s, geometric forms, Chrysler Building, machine-age glamour
